history: Causal Loop Diagram (8-10 variables with loops and delays) → Customer service representatives (high routine tasks, clear automation trajectory) → Focus on systemic feedback (AI capability, adoption rate, worker displacement, political pressure, regulation) as self-reinforcing cycle
description: This project models customer service work using a causal loop diagram that traces how AI capability, adoption rates, worker displacement, and political pressure create reinforcing feedback cycles. The core mechanism shows how faster AI adoption increases layoffs, which generates political pressure for regulation that can either slow or accelerate further automation depending on whether worker support policies exist. The two scenarios compare an unregulated high-automation path against one where strong retraining and income support policies dampen the feedback loop and stabilize employment.
consultant_notes: Strong job choice (customer service reps have clear automation pressure and policy responses) and loop selection (capability→adoption→displacement→regulation forms natural cycle). Risk: ensure you explicitly model the *difference* between high automation pressure and high worker support scenarios as distinct parameter sets or loop strength adjustments in a single diagram, not two separate diagrams—this keeps the 1-figure constraint tight while clarifying causal contrast.
anand_notes: Clearly doable; PROBABLY TOO EASY FOR 7 DAYS

history: Causal Loop Diagram (8-10 variables with loops and delays) → Software developers (moderate automation pressure, skill-shift dynamics) → Broad focus: AI disrupting multiple dev roles (juniors, QA, DevOps, technical leads through different pressures)
description: This project models software development through a causal loop diagram tracking how AI automation, developer reskilling, and hiring patterns reinforce each other across different role types (juniors, QA, DevOps, leads). The core mechanism is a feedback loop where AI adoption reduces demand for routine coding tasks, which triggers either workforce contraction or upskilling investment depending on organizational response. The two scenarios compare outcomes under high automation pressure (minimal retraining budget, rapid tool adoption) versus high worker support (significant upskilling programs, gradual adoption), showing how each pathway affects employment levels and skill composition differently over time.
consultant_notes: The CLD choice is sound and software developers is a well-motivated domain with clear AI disruption dynamics. However, the plan conflates "broad focus across multiple dev roles" with the core requirement to compare exactly two scenarios (high automation vs. high support)—this tension needs immediate clarification. The student should pick one dev role (e.g., junior developers OR QA engineers, not all three) as the focal unit, then build an 8–10 variable CLD that models how that role responds differently under automation-pressure vs. worker-support scenarios. Currently the scope risks becoming either too fragmented (trying to model 3+ roles simultaneously) or too vague (unclear which role anchors the comparison). Recommend: (1) explicitly name the single job category in focus, (2) map out the two scenario conditions as parameter/initial-condition changes (e.g., "training budget" or "automation investment rate"), and (3) draft variable list (~8–10) with clear feedback loops before day 3 to validate feasibility.
anand_notes: SAME AS ABOVE; COULD BE SOMEWHAT MORE COMPLEX THAN CALL CENTER

history: Agent-Based Simulation (15-20 workers with states and emergence) → Software developers (mixed routine/creative work, visible skill stratification) → Use NetLogo (visual, built-in UI, easier scenario comparison, exports to screenshot)
description: The project builds an agent-based simulation in NetLogo with 15–20 software developers whose skills and employment status evolve based on AI tool adoption and job market competition. The core mechanism tracks how AI availability either raises or lowers the bar for staying employed, creating emergent patterns of skill stratification where some developers upskill quickly while others fall behind or leave the workforce. The two scenarios contrast a "high automation pressure" case (AI tools proliferate rapidly, junior roles disappear) against a "high worker support" case (companies invest in retraining programs and retain developers across skill levels), with side-by-side NetLogo screenshots showing the resulting workforce composition over time.
consultant_notes: NetLogo is an excellent fit for the ABM track and software developers offer rich stratification dynamics; the main risk is ensuring emergent behavior is truly distinct from programmed rules (e.g., skill clustering must arise from interactions, not be hard-coded). The 7-day timeline is comfortable if you prototype scenarios by day 3 and reserve days 4-6 for refinement and memo writing.
anand_notes: DOABLE & ALSO APPROPRIATE SCOPE FOR A WEEK

history: Agent-Based Simulation (15-20 workers with states and emergence) → Software developers (mixed routine/creative work, visible skill stratification) → Use a spreadsheet-based simulation (Excel/Sheets with VBA or formulas, fastest prototyping, limited emergence complexity)
description: This project models software developer workforce dynamics using a spreadsheet-based agent simulation with 15–20 individual workers whose skills, wages, and employment status evolve monthly based on AI tool adoption and retraining opportunities. The core mechanism is skill-based stratification: routine coding tasks automated by AI compress wages and employability for mid-tier developers, while senior architects and junior learners either thrive (through tool leverage) or survive (through mentorship), creating emergent inequality within the cohort. The two scenarios differ in policy pressure—high automation (firms aggressively adopt AI, minimal retraining support) versus high support (subsidized upskilling programs, slower AI rollout)—to show how workforce outcomes diverge under each pressure regime.
consultant_notes: The software developer choice is sharp and domain-appropriate for visible stratification and AI impact, and ABS is the right method. However, spreadsheet-based simulation will severely constrain emergence—you'll struggle to model the interaction dynamics (skill clustering, team bottlenecks, knowledge transfer breakdown) that make the comparison meaningful. Consider a lightweight Python sim (Mesa or custom loops) instead, which still prototypes quickly but lets you implement 1–2 genuinely emergent mechanisms (e.g., knowledge hoarding when job security drops, skill bifurcation). This gives you competitive advantage in interpretation without timeline risk.
anand_notes: EXCEL/VBA MAY NOT BE THE RIGHT TOOL; LOWER SCOPE FIT AS SOMEONE COULD SPEND MORE TIME TO GET EVERYTHING WORKING
